docs.title: 🎬 Welcome to YouWiki
docs.text:
Documentation companion for video tutorials across multiple projects

YouWiki is a comprehensive documentation platform designed to complement video content from across my projects. Rather than transcripts or scripts, you'll find practical, structured guides that let you follow along with videos, copy-paste code examples, and reference key concepts without constantly rewinding.

docs.title: 🎯 What is YouWiki?
docs.text:
This site serves as a central hub for documentation, tutorials, and guides covering various topics and projects. Whether you're learning a new framework, setting up infrastructure, or building a project from scratch, you'll find:

  • Step-by-step guides to follow alongside video tutorials
  • Code snippets & examples ready to use in your projects
  • Architectural overviews explaining design decisions
  • Configuration references and troubleshooting tips
  • Best practices & lessons learned from real-world implementations

docs.title: 📂 Available Channels
docs.text:
Explore different areas of focus by browsing the channels in the navigation menu:

  • Engineering Channel - Tutorials on building, deploying, and automating projects using modern tech stacks
  • More channels coming soon...

docs.title: 💡 How to Use This Site
docs.text:
1. Pick a channel - Browse the navigation to find the topic area you're interested in
  2. Watch the video - Follow along with the corresponding YouTube tutorial
  3. Reference the docs - Use this site as a companion to copy code, check configurations, and understand key concepts
  4. Learn at your pace - Revisit sections anytime for reference or deeper understanding

docs.title: 🔍 Finding What You Need
docs.text:
• Top navigation - Select a channel to explore
  • Left sidebar - Browse specific tutorials and topics within each channel
  • Search - Use the search functionality to find topics across all content

Made for learners who prefer building over just watching. 🏗️

docs.title: Engineering Channel
docs.text: Welcome to the Engineering Channel – a collection of practical tutorials focused on building, deploying, and automating real-world projects.

docs.title: 📚 YouTube Bot Series: Build an AI YouTube Bot with MLflow & GitHub Actions
docs.text: A comprehensive 5-part series taking you from getting raw materials to running a production-ready AI bot.

docs.title: Series Overview
docs.text: # Series Focus Status 1 Interacting with YouTube via API Getting the raw materials (GCP, credentials, API calls) ✅ Ready 2 Skeleton of the Bot Building the core loop (parsing, state management, dummy replies) ✅ Ready 3 Prompt Engineering & MLflow Giving the bot a brain (LLM integration, experiment tracking) ✅ Ready 4 GitHub Actions: The DevOps Way Automating execution (scheduling, CI/CD, secrets) ✅ Ready 5 The System as a Whole Production readiness (testing, monitoring, error handling) ✅ Ready

docs.title: Why This Structure?
docs.text:
We've designed these series to build progressively: 1. Start simple - Just talk to YouTube's API 2. Add structure - Organize code and manage state 3. Add intelligence - Make the bot actually smart 4. Automate it - Run without manual intervention 5. Make it robust - Handle failures gracefully

Each series builds on the previous one, and you can watch the corresponding YouTube video to follow along.

docs.title: MLflow for ML Dev
docs.text:
Deep dive into MLflow, the open-source platform for managing machine learning workflows.

Perfect for: - Tracking experiments and hyperparameter tuning - Managing model versions and transitions - Setting up model registries - Integrating tracking into existing pipelines

docs.title: 🎯 How to Navigate
docs.text:
1. Start with Series 1 - Go through them in order to understand the complete flow
  2. Or jump around - Each series is independent and can be used for reference
  3. Follow the videos - Watch each YouTube video alongside the corresponding series
  4. Use as reference - Come back to copy code snippets or check configurations

docs.title: 💡 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Basic Python knowledge
  • Comfortable with APIs and HTTP requests
  • (Optional) Familiarity with machine learning concepts helps for Series 3+

Ready to build? Start with Series 1: Interacting with YouTube via API 🚀

docs.title: Series 1: Interacting with YouTube via API
🎯 Focus: Getting the Raw Materials
docs.text: In this series, we'll learn how to interact with the YouTube API to fetch the data we need for our bot. This is the foundation - we're getting the raw materials that our bot will work with.

docs.title: Setting up GCP Credentials
docs.text:
• Create a Google Cloud Project
  • Enable YouTube Data API v3
  • Generate OAuth 2.0 credentials
  • Store and manage API keys securely

docs.title: Fetching Comments
docs.text:
• Query YouTube video comments using the API
  • Filter and parse comment data
  • Handle pagination for large comment sets
  • Best practices for API rate limiting

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• Implement OAuth 2.0 authentication flow
  • Handle refresh tokens
  • Manage token expiration and renewal

docs.title: Posting a Test Reply
docs.text:
• Structure reply payloads
  • Post your first programmatic reply to a YouTube comment
  • Handle success and error responses

docs.title: 🚀 What You'll Build
docs.text: By the end of this series, you'll have: - ✅ A working GCP setup with YouTube API access - ✅ Code that fetches comments from a target video - ✅ Authentication flow that handles credentials - ✅ Ability to post test replies programmatically

docs.title: 📝 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Basic Python knowledge
  • Google account with ability to create GCP projects
  • Understanding of APIs and HTTP requests

docs.title: 🎬 Watch the Video Series
docs.text:
Follow along with the YouTube videos and use this guide to: - Copy code snippets - Reference configuration steps - Troubleshoot common issues

Next Step: Follow the video to set up your GCP project, then return here for code examples and detailed explanations.

docs.title: 🎯 Focus: Building the Core Loop
docs.text: Now that we have the ability to fetch data from YouTube, let's build the core structure of our bot. Think of this as creating the skeleton - the fundamental architecture that everything else will hang on.

docs.title: Structuring Code Cleanly
docs.text:
• Organize code into modules and classes
  • Separation of concerns (API interaction, comment parsing, reply logic)
  • Project structure best practices
  • Configuration management

docs.title: Parsing Fetched Comments
docs.text:
• Extract relevant information from raw comment data
  • Filter comments (by date, video, user, etc.)
  • Handle different comment formats and edge cases
  • Clean and normalize data

docs.title: Setting up State Management
docs.text:
• Database vs. local state approaches
  • Choosing the right persistence strategy
  • Schema design for tracking processed comments
  • Preventing duplicate replies

docs.title: Writing a Dummy Text Replier
docs.text:
• Create a simple rule-based reply generator
  • Test the reply pipeline end-to-end
  • Placeholder for future AI-powered replies
  • Error handling and logging

docs.title: 🚀 What You'll Build
docs.text: By the end of this series, you'll have: - ✅ A well-structured bot application - ✅ Comment parsing and filtering logic - ✅ State management system to track processed comments - ✅ A complete (albeit simple) bot loop that runs without errors

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture Overview
docs.text:
YouTube API
    ↓
Fetch Comments
    ↓
Parse & Filter
    ↓
Check State (Already Replied?)
    ↓
Generate Reply (Dummy)
    ↓
Post Reply
    ↓
Update State

docs.title: 📝 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Completion of Series 1 (YouTube API Setup)
  • Comfortable with Python data structures
  • Basic database or file I/O knowledge

docs.title: 🎬 Watch & Follow Along
docs.text:
This series builds directly on Series 1. Keep your API credentials handy and follow the video to implement each component.

Next Step: After completing this series, you'll have a working bot skeleton. In Series 3, we'll replace the dummy replies with AI-powered responses.

docs.title: 🎯 Focus: Giving the Bot a "Brain"
docs.text: Now we're going to give our bot intelligence! We'll integrate an LLM API and set up MLflow to track and optimize the bot's responses through prompt engineering.

docs.title: Integrating the LLM API
docs.text:
• Choosing an LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, etc.)
  • Setting up API authentication and credentials
  • Understanding token limits and costs
  • Error handling and rate limiting

docs.title: Replacing Dummy Replies
docs.text:
• Replace the simple rule-based replier with LLM calls
  • Design prompts that work well for YouTube comments
  • Handle different types of comments intelligently
  • Ensure responses are appropriate and on-brand

docs.title: Setting up MLflow
docs.text:
• Install and configure MLflow
  • Understanding experiments and runs
  • Logging prompts, parameters, and metrics
  • Comparing different approaches

docs.title: Logging Prompt Experiments
docs.text:
• Structure your experiments for reproducibility
  • Log prompt variations
  • Track response quality metrics
  • Monitor performance over time

docs.title: Versioning the Best-Performing Prompts
docs.text:
• Register models in MLflow
  • Create model stages (Staging, Production)
  • Transition prompts as you improve them
  • Rollback to previous versions if needed

docs.title: 🚀 What You'll Build
docs.text: By the end of this series, you'll have: - ✅ An AI-powered bot that generates intelligent replies - ✅ MLflow tracking your experiments - ✅ A versioning system for prompt management - ✅ The ability to compare and optimize prompts data-driven way

docs.title: 🧪 Experiment Loop
docs.text:
Design Prompt
    ↓
Run in MLflow Experiment
    ↓
Evaluate Responses
    ↓
Log Metrics & Results
    ↓
Compare Against Baseline
    ↓
Promote Best to Production

docs.title: 📝 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Completion of Series 2 (Bot Skeleton)
  • Understanding of LLM APIs and prompt design
  • Familiarity with experiment tracking concepts

docs.title: 💡 Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Prompt Engineering: Crafting prompts to get better responses from the LLM
  • Experiment Tracking: Recording all trials and their outcomes
  • Model Registry: Maintaining versions of your best prompts/settings
  • Reproducibility: Being able to recreate any previous result

docs.title: 🎬 Watch & Follow Along
docs.text:
Follow the video as we integrate GPT/LLM API and set up MLflow tracking. Use this guide for: - Code snippets and API examples - MLflow configuration - Prompt templates to start with

Next Step: Once your AI-powered bot is working well and tracked in MLflow, Series 4 will automate its execution using GitHub Actions.

docs.title: Series 4: GitHub Actions - The DevOps Way
🎯 Focus: Automating the Execution
docs.text: Your bot works great when you run it manually, but real automation means it runs on schedule without any intervention. We'll set up GitHub Actions to make this happen!

docs.title: Writing YAML Workflows
docs.text:
• Understanding GitHub Actions structure
  • Triggers (schedule, on-push, manual, etc.)
  • Job and step definitions
  • Using actions from the marketplace

docs.title: Managing Secrets Securely
docs.text:
• Storing API keys and credentials securely
  • GitHub Secrets vault
  • Accessing secrets in your workflows
  • Best practices for secret management

docs.title: Setting up Cron Jobs
docs.text:
• Scheduling your bot to run at specific times
  • Cron syntax and timing
  • Running multiple times per day or specific days
  • Handling timezones properly

docs.title: Error Handling & Notifications
docs.text:
• Catching and logging errors in workflows
  • Sending notifications on failure
  • Retry strategies
  • Debugging failed runs

docs.title: 🚀 What You'll Build
docs.text: By the end of this series, you'll have: - ✅ A GitHub Actions workflow that runs your bot automatically - ✅ Secure credential management in your CI/CD pipeline - ✅ A bot that replies to comments on a schedule - ✅ Notifications when something goes wrong

docs.title: 🔄 Workflow Architecture
docs.text:
Scheduled Trigger (Cron)
    ↓
Checkout Repository
    ↓
Setup Python Environment
    ↓
Install Dependencies
    ↓
Run Bot Script
    ↓
Log Results & Errors
    ↓
Notify on Failure

docs.title: 📝 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Completion of Series 3 (Prompt Engineering & MLflow)
  • Basic understanding of CI/CD concepts
  • Git and GitHub account knowledge

docs.title: 📋 Workflow Checklist
docs.text:
• ✓ Create .github/workflows/ directory
  • ✓ Define schedule trigger (cron)
  • ✓ Set up secrets for API keys
  • ✓ Install dependencies
  • ✓ Run bot with MLflow logging
  • ✓ Add error handling and notifications

docs.title: 🎬 Watch & Follow Along
docs.text: Follow the video to set up your first automated workflow. Use this guide for: - Workflow YAML templates - Secrets configuration - Cron expression examples - Troubleshooting common issues

docs.title: 💡 Common Cron Expressions
docs.text:
# Every hour
0 * * * *

# Every 6 hours
0 */6 * * *

# Every day at 9 AM UTC
0 9 * * *

# Every Monday at 8 AM UTC
0 8 * * 1

# Every 30 minutes
*/30 * * * *

Next Step: Your bot is now fully automated! In Series 5, we'll look at the complete system end-to-end and add monitoring and error handling.

docs.title: Series 5: The System as a Whole
🎯 Focus: Tying It All Together
docs.text: We've built all the pieces - now let's ensure they work together reliably in production. This series focuses on robustness, monitoring, and maintaining a live system.

docs.title: End-to-End Testing
docs.text:
• Integration testing across all components
  • Testing the complete comment → reply flow
  • Mocking YouTube API for testing
  • Test data strategies and fixtures
  • CI/CD testing integration

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• Handling API rate limits gracefully
  • Retry strategies with exponential backoff
  • Handling network failures
  • Graceful degradation
  • Logging errors effectively

docs.title: Monitoring the Live System
docs.text:
• Setting up logging infrastructure
  • Monitoring MLflow experiment results
  • Tracking API usage and costs
  • Alerts for failures or anomalies
  • Dashboard creation for visibility

docs.title: Handling Edge Cases
docs.text:
• Comments from blocked users
  • Deleted videos or comments
  • API quota exceeded scenarios
  • LLM API failures
  • Database connection issues

docs.title: 🚀 What You'll Build
docs.text: By the end of this series, you'll have: - ✅ A robust bot that handles failures gracefully - ✅ End-to-end tests ensuring reliability - ✅ Monitoring and alerting in place - ✅ Comprehensive error logging - ✅ A production-ready system

docs.title: 🛡️ Robustness Architecture
docs.text:
Request from Scheduled Job
    ↓
Try Main Logic
    ├─ Fetch Comments
    ├─ Parse & Filter
    ├─ Call LLM
    ├─ Post Reply
    └─ Update State
    ↓
Catch & Log Errors
    ├─ Rate Limit? → Retry Later
    ├─ API Down? → Log & Continue
    ├─ LLM Failed? → Skip & Log
    └─ DB Error? → Alert & Investigate
    ↓
Log Success/Failure
    ↓
Send Metrics to MLflow
    ↓
Alert if Necessary

docs.title: 📊 Monitoring Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Error rate tracking
  • [ ] API response times
  • [ ] Cost per run
  • [ ] Successful replies count
  • [ ] Failed API calls
  • [ ] Database operation times
  • [ ] LLM quality metrics

docs.title: 🔄 Typical Production Issues
docs.text: Issue Solution YouTube API rate limited Implement backoff and batching LLM API costs spiraling Add prompt caching or rate limiting Database connection drops Connection pooling and retry logic Comments deleted before reply Check before posting Tokens expiring Automatic refresh token handling Memory leaks Proper resource cleanup

docs.title: 📝 Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Completion of Series 1-4 (All previous series)
  • Understanding of production systems
  • Familiarity with logging and monitoring

docs.title: 🎬 Watch & Follow Along
docs.text: Follow the video as we add production-ready features. Use this guide for: - Error handling code patterns - Logging configuration examples - Testing strategies and test templates - Monitoring setup instructions

docs.title: 🎓 Post-Completion: Scaling & Optimization
docs.text:
After completing this series, consider: - Scaling to multiple videos - Improving prompt quality with more experiments - Cost optimization strategies - Community contributions and open-sourcing - Documentation for other developers

Congratulations! 🎉 You've built a complete, production-ready YouTube bot with experiment tracking, automation, and monitoring. This foundation can be expanded and improved indefinitely.
